items.q: Are the internet and the World Wide Web the same thing?
items.correct: No - the internet is the global physical network of networks; the web is one service running on it, alongside email, gaming and video calls.
items.misconception: Yes - internet and web are two words for the same system.
items.random_wrong: Yes - turning off websites would turn off the internet.
items.why: Daily usage treats them as synonyms. The layering matters in exams: the web (HTTP) is an application running over the internet's transport infrastructure.

items.q: Is a wireless connection always faster than a wired one?
items.correct: No - cabled connections are generally faster and more reliable; wireless trades speed and stability for convenience and mobility.
items.misconception: Yes - Wi-Fi is newer technology, so it must be faster.
items.random_wrong: Yes - cables slow signals down because of friction.
items.why: Newer-equals-faster intuition misleads. Interference, distance and shared airspace degrade wireless, which is why servers and gamers stay wired.

items.q: If one computer in a star network fails, does the whole network go down?
items.correct: No - only that device is affected; the network fails as a whole only if the CENTRAL switch or hub fails, which is the topology's weak point.
items.misconception: Yes - any single failure brings down every device in a star network.
items.random_wrong: Yes - star networks shut down for safety whenever a device leaves.
items.why: Students misplace the single point of failure. The radial layout isolates leaf failures but concentrates risk in the centre - a classic compare-and-contrast point.

items.q: In a star topology, all devices are connected to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A central switch or hub. “Each other in a ring” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A phone connected to wireless earbuds over Bluetooth forms a:
items.why: Correct answer: PAN. “WAN” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A WAN is best described as:
items.correct: A network connecting computers or LANs across a large geographical area
items.misconception: A network within a single room only
items.random_wrong: A network with no cables anywhere
items.why: Correct answer: A network connecting computers or LANs across a large geographical area. “A network within a single room only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Compared with HTTP, HTTPS additionally provides:
items.correct: Encryption of the data exchanged with the website
items.why: It's Encryption of the data exchanged with the website. “Faster downloads in all cases” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many layers are in the TCP/IP model (application, transport, internet, link)?
items.why: The real figure is 4 layers. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
